title: Peri-event time histogram (trial-aligned event counts)

purpose.canonicalQuestion: How does the event count of a declared selected-sender analysis vary with time relative to a declared per-trial alignment event, using exact integer counts and explicit trial and sender cardinality denominators? Events mode retains the selected-sender identities; prebinned mode retains only aggregate cardinality.
cannotEstablish: That the declared alignment times mark the event they are labelled with. Cortexel aligns to the times it is given; it cannot verify that they are stimulus onsets rather than trial starts, or that they were corrected for display latency.

That a modulation around the alignment instant was CAUSED by the aligning event. A PSTH shows temporal coincidence with a declared reference; a stimulus-locked artifact, a periodic background, or a slow drift across the session produce the same shape.

That a response is time-locked rather than latency-jittered. Averaging over trials attenuates a response whose latency varies from trial to trial, so a flat PSTH is not evidence that no response occurred.

That any bin differs significantly from baseline. This revision renders counts and exact derived values but accepts no uncertainty marks. It performs no test, applies no multiple-comparison correction across bins, and reports no p-value.

The instantaneous rate. A PSTH is an exact count in a finite bin: the height of a peak depends on the bin width that was chosen, and this contract offers no kernel estimate of an underlying intensity function.

The response of senders that were not selected, or of trials that were excluded. Every value is computed over the caller-declared included-trial and selected-sender scope and says nothing about anything outside it. In prebinned mode Cortexel retains only exact scope cardinalities, not selected-sender identities or the included/excluded trial membership partition.
That a bin no included trial covered has a rate of zero. That bin was not observed. It is reported as null, drawn as a gap, and never painted as a measured zero.

normalization: Recorder output is NOT assumed chronological, grouped by trial, or grouped by sender. Binning is order-independent and processes every event exactly once in source order; no sort is required or claimed. The canonical request and derivation input digest bind that original order, while the emitted table is deliberately bin-level rather than a second event-list surface.

Relative time is t_event minus the alignment time of the event's own trial. ABSOLUTE event times are required; pre-aligned relative times are not accepted, because a caller-computed relative time cannot be checked against the trial's alignment time. Event and alignment units may differ; both must be of the time dimension, are converted through the typed unit layer, and the conversion is disclosed.

The relative window decides MEMBERSHIP; the bins decide PLACEMENT, and membership is decided first. Under an explicitly declared half-open window [start, stop) an event at exactly relStop is out of window: it is excluded, counted, and disclosed. `finalEdgeInclusive` cannot resurrect it. A closed [start, stop] window instead requires a closed bin boundary and final-edge inclusion.
Without that precedence a spike at exactly relStop would be counted in the final bin of this epoch and again in the first bin of an adjacent epoch that begins there. One spike, two counts.

When relative time 0 is an included start or internal edge, an event exactly at the alignment instant belongs to the bin that BEGINS at 0, not the preceding bin that ends there. If zero is the stop endpoint, membership precedence applies: a half-open window excludes it, while a closed window places it in the inclusive final bin. This is stated because an off-by-one at zero can move a response into the baseline.

Bins must tile the relative window exactly after each window endpoint is correctly rounded once through the typed unit conversion into the bin unit. No extent-scaled tolerance is used: on a huge window such a tolerance could hide a real gap near zero. An untiled window would let an in-window event land in no bin and vanish without a count.

There is exactly ONE authoritative bin-edge vector, at `/parameters/bins`, in BOTH modes. A pre-binned request declares the bins it used there; it does not carry a second edge vector that could disagree with the analysis binning.
Duplicate identical event rows are PRESERVED and counted. Two records of the same spike are a source-data question, not something the renderer may quietly resolve.
Trial ids are unique. A duplicate trial id is refused before it can diverge alignment (which alignment time wins?) and the denominator (is the trial counted once or twice?).

A bin that no included trial covers has NO observation. Its count and its value are null — never 0, never interpolated across, never bridged by a step. MISSING_VALUES_PRESENT reports how many such bins exist.
There is no default normalization and no default denominator policy. Both are required: choosing them is a scientific claim, and a default would make that claim on the caller's behalf.

JSON negative zero is normalized to canonical zero at the snapshot boundary and cannot remain a distinct scientific value or digest spelling. Unit aliases are refused with a repair; a stable request carries only canonical codes.
derivation: count: in events mode, for each half-open bin, the exact number of in-window events that come from a declared trial and selected sender and whose relative time falls in that bin. In prebinned mode, the supplied exact count asserts the declared coverage. Counts are exact non-negative integers. A null count is permitted only in prebinned mode and means the bin was covered by no included trial.

Denominator, `uniform_trial_count`: every bin's denominator is includedTrialCount. Every included trial is thereby ASSERTED to have observed the entire relative window. Events mode has no partial-coverage carrier in revision 2, so all declared trials are included under this assertion.

Denominator, `per_bin_covering_trials`: revision 2 accepts this policy only in prebinned mode, where every bin supplies its exact positive covering-trial count (or null together with a null count when no trial covered it). Events mode has no trial-coverage carrier and is rejected structurally under this policy.

Cortexel will NOT infer a per-bin coverage denominator from which trials happened to spike. A no-spike trial may still cover the bin completely, so event presence cannot establish exposure. Supply exact prebinned counts and denominators, or use events with the uniform full-window assertion.
count_per_trial = count / trialDenominator(bin). Dimensionless (kind `ratio`, unit `1`). It carries no per-neuron claim.

total_event_rate_per_trial = count / (trialDenominator(bin) * binWidth), expressed in the declared frequency unit (Hz when no prebinned `/data/rates` unit is supplied). This is the event rate of the whole selected group per trial. It carries NO per-neuron claim and is the honest choice when the selected senders are not the population you want to speak about.

mean_rate_per_selected_sender_per_trial = count / (trialDenominator(bin) * recordedSenderCount * binWidth), expressed in the declared frequency unit (Hz when no prebinned `/data/rates` unit is supplied). This IS a per-neuron claim. Time and frequency units are reconciled through the typed unit layer, never through a hard-coded factor of 1000.

The per-selected-sender normalization additionally requires `senderExposurePolicy: all_selected_senders_cover_every_counted_trial_bin`. This is an explicit rectangular-exposure assertion, not something event presence can prove: a silent sender may have been observed, while a disconnected or late-added sender may not have been observable. The receipt retains the assertion and RECTANGULAR_SENDER_EXPOSURE_ASSERTED states that Cortexel could not verify it.

Baseline (rate normalizations only): baselineRate = (sum of counts over the baseline bins) / (sum over the baseline bins of trialDenominator * binWidth_in_seconds, times recordedSenderCount for the per-sender normalization). The baseline is re-derived from raw counts and exposures as ONE aggregate interval.

The baseline is NOT the arithmetic mean of the per-bin rates. That mean would weight a narrow or thinly covered bin as heavily as a wide, fully covered one, and would change value if the same data were binned differently.

The corrected value is rate(bin) minus baselineRate. It is a signed difference and may be negative. The raw count and the uncorrected rate are ALWAYS retained in the artifact and the table: the correction adds a column, it never replaces the count.

Baseline correction is formed as one signed exact rational difference between the bin count/exposure and aggregate baseline count/exposure, then rounded once to binary64. Cortexel does not subtract two separately rounded rates: at large counts that can change a one-event contrast from 1/3 to 1/2.

A baseline rate of exactly zero in the declared frequency unit is legitimate — subtracting zero is well defined. A baseline whose total exposure is zero is not: no included trial covered the baseline window, so no baseline exists, and the request is refused with SCIENCE_DENOMINATOR_INVALID rather than fabricating one.

Each baseline-window bound must identify exactly one bin edge after one correctly rounded typed conversion into the bin unit. This permits physically intended endpoints written in another registered time unit without applying an extent-scaled tolerance; zero matches, ambiguous matches, and reversed edges are refused. A baseline that cuts a bin in half has no defined exposure for that bin.

Supplied `/data/rates.values` are RE-DERIVED in `/data/rates.unit` from the counts, the per-bin denominators, the selected-sender count when applicable, and the bin widths. Supplied `count` values require exact non-negative safe-integer equality. Other normalized values must be finite and non-negative and are compared with a per-value relative tolerance of 1e-12 against that bin's exact derived magnitude; a derived zero requires exact zero. A large peak can never hide an error in a small bin.

Cortexel never recovers a count from a rate; `counts` is required in prebinned mode. A rate is a lossy encoding of a count: at 4 trials x 5 senders x a 10 ms bin, one event is 5 Hz, so a supplied 12 Hz implies 2.4 events — a number no recorder ever produced. Refusing to invert is cheaper than deciding which rounding is honest.

Events mode has no trial-exclusion carrier in revision 2: every id in `trialIds` is included and its event rows enter the same uniform denominator. Prebinned mode alone carries includedTrialCount and excludedTrialCount; the producer asserts that excluded trials entered neither supplied counts nor denominators, Cortexel checks the exact cardinality identity, and PRE_BINNED_INPUT states that the membership partition cannot be recovered.

Bins are drawn as literal steps or bars on their exact interval boundaries. A vertical reference is drawn when relative time 0 lies on the displayed axis. The summary distinguishes an included zero from zero at a half-open stop boundary, where the line is visible but events exactly at zero are excluded. A strictly pre-only or post-only axis states that the alignment instant is out of view. When a baseline is applied, a horizontal signed-value zero line remains visible. This contract has NO kernel mode.

Revision 2 accepts only an explicit uncertainty `{kind: none, reason}` declaration and draws no uncertainty mark. Cortexel never invents a sqrt(N) Poisson band: that band is a model assumption about the spike-generating process, not an observation, and on the figure it would be indistinguishable from measured across-trial dispersion. A future uncertainty variant is a contract extension and must ship with matching geometry, table fields, summaries, and tests.

Complexity: O(T) to index the trials and O(n log B) to place n events into B explicit or materialized bins. Prebinned normalization and exact exposure audit are O(B). No event sort or trial-by-bin coverage expansion is performed in revision 2.
scopeRules: The trial universe must be declared and non-empty, and at least one trial must be included. Trial count is never inferred from the maximum trial id or from the trials that happen to appear in the event list: a trial in which nothing happened is still a trial.

In events mode the selected sender universe (`recordedSenderIds`) must be declared and non-empty and must include selected senders that never fired. In prebinned mode only its exact positive cardinality (`recordedSenderCount`) remains observable. Both are per-neuron denominator authorities and neither is inferred from event presence.

In events mode every event's sender must be a member of `recordedSenderIds`, and every event's trial must be a member of the declared trial universe `trialIds`. An event from a sender or a trial that was supposedly not recorded is a contradiction, not a rounding error. Prebinned mode exposes no event identities, so PRE_BINNED_INPUT discloses that membership cannot be independently checked.

`ids.unique` is applied to the flat arrays that exist in a branch: `/data/recordedSenderIds` in events mode and `/data/trialIds` in both modes. The trial universe and its per-trial `alignmentTimes` are positionally parallel columnar arrays rather than a row table, so a duplicate sender or trial id fails before it can diverge selection, alignment, or the denominator.

In prebinned mode Cortexel cannot see the trial coverage. It checks that no per-bin denominator exceeds includedTrialCount, that a count and its denominator are null together, and it re-derives every value — but it cannot verify that a denominator of 3 really corresponds to three trials that covered that bin. PRE_BINNED_INPUT discloses exactly this.

Prebinned mode retains `recordedSenderCount`, `includedTrialCount`, and `excludedTrialCount` as exact cardinalities. It does not carry selected-sender ids or identify which `trialIds` belong to the included versus excluded partition. Those memberships cannot be reconstructed from aggregate counts, and PRE_BINNED_INPUT discloses that loss rather than implying identity-level traceability.

Under `uniform_trial_count` every bin must be observed and its denominator must EQUAL includedTrialCount, in both modes. A prebinned request with a null or varying denominator contradicts the policy's assertion that every included trial observed the whole window and is refused with SCIENCE_DENOMINATOR_INVALID rather than one of the two being silently believed.

The figure makes no claim beyond its declared scope. Events mode carries the selected-sender and included-trial identity universes. Prebinned mode carries only exact selected-sender, included-trial, and excluded-trial cardinalities, not those membership sets. Neither is a claim about a wider population or session.

eventTimes.description: ABSOLUTE event times on the recording clock. Pre-aligned relative times are not accepted: alignment is the operation this figure exists to perform, and a caller-computed relative time cannot be checked against the trial's alignment time.

eventTrialIds.description: The trial each event belongs to. It selects the alignment time; an event with no trial has no relative time. Read against `trialIds` by events.trial_universe_declared, so every value must be a member of the declared trial universe.

recordedSenderIds.description: The COMPLETE set of senders that were recorded AND selected — including any that never fired in any trial. This is the per-neuron denominator basis and it cannot be inferred from the events. events.sender_universe_declared requires every event sender to be a member.

trialIds.description: The COMPLETE trial universe as a flat array, including trials in which nothing happened. Declared, never inferred from the observed event trial ids: a trial with no events is still a trial. Kept unique by ids.unique and used as the universe by events.trial_universe_declared.

alignmentUnit.description: The unit of every entry in `alignmentTimes`. Alignment times are on the same absolute clock as `eventTimes` and must share its dimension; a differing code is converted through the typed unit layer and the conversion is disclosed.

alignmentTimes.description: The absolute alignment time of each trial, positionally parallel to `trialIds`. Relative time is t_event minus the alignment time of the event's own trial. Required by psth.alignment_declared: without it there is nothing for time zero to mean.

relativeWindow.description: The analysis window in RELATIVE time, measured from each trial's own alignment time. It decides membership; the bins decide placement. window.valid reads it at /data/relativeWindow.

trialIds.description: The COMPLETE trial universe associated with the pre-binned analysis, as a flat array. It is includedTrialCount + excludedTrialCount long, but revision 2 does not identify which members are included versus excluded; only the exact partition cardinalities remain. Kept unique by ids.unique; its length is the trial count read by psth.alignment_declared.

alignmentUnit.description: The unit of every entry in `alignmentTimes`.

alignmentTimes.description: The absolute alignment time of each trial in `trialIds`, positionally parallel to it. A pre-binned request still declares the reference it was aligned to; psth.alignment_declared requires it in both modes.

counts.description: Producer-supplied exact integer event counts per bin over the aggregate scope described only by included-trial and selected-sender cardinalities. Cortexel range-checks them and re-derives normalization but cannot re-derive them from raw observations. `null` means the producer declares that NO included trial covered the bin: it is a hole, not a measured zero. Length must be one less than the authoritative edge count.

trialDenominators.description: The number of included trials that FULLY cover each bin. Under a uniform denominator this is the same positive integer in every bin. It is null exactly where `counts` is null: a count without a denominator, or a denominator without a count, is a contradiction and is refused.

recordedSenderCount.description: The number of senders that were recorded and selected, including any that never fired. This exact cardinality is the per-neuron denominator. Prebinned mode does not retain their identities, so PRE_BINNED_INPUT discloses that membership cannot be recovered. rate.denominator_positive requires a positive integer.

includedTrialCount.description: The exact number of trials included in the aggregate analysis. No per-bin denominator may exceed it. The identities of the included members of `trialIds` are not retained in revision 2.

excludedTrialCount.description: The exact number of trials deliberately excluded from the aggregate analysis. Declared, never inferred: the trial-universe cardinality is includedTrialCount + excludedTrialCount, but revision 2 does not retain the membership partition.

rates.description: Optional. The pre-normalized values per bin. Its `kind` must match the requested normalization: `count` -> count, `count_per_trial` -> ratio, either rate -> firing_rate. `null` exactly where `counts` is null.

relativeWindow.description: The exact relative membership window used by the aggregate. Its boundary is required explicitly; PSTH does not inherit the common-schema display default.

alignmentLabel.description: What the alignment times are DECLARED to be, for example "Stimulus onset". It labels the zero line. Cortexel cannot verify it: the scientific fact is the per-trial alignment time, and the label is a caller declaration like the source system.

bins.description: The single authoritative bin-edge vector, in RELATIVE time, in BOTH modes. The bins must tile the relative window exactly. Boundary fields are not redundant alternatives here: `[start,stop)` requires `[lo,hi)` plus `finalEdgeInclusive:false`, while `[start,stop]` requires `[lo,hi]` plus `finalEdgeInclusive:true`; contradictory requested versus effective rules are refused. Cortexel never chooses a bin width for you: the width determines the height of every peak in the figure.

normalization.description: `count` is the exact integer. `count_per_trial` divides by the bin's trial denominator. `total_event_rate_per_trial` is the rate of the whole selected group per trial and carries NO per-neuron claim. `mean_rate_per_selected_sender_per_trial` additionally divides by the selected-sender count and IS a per-neuron claim. The two rates are separate ids precisely so they cannot be conflated.

denominatorPolicy.description: `uniform_trial_count` asserts that every included trial observed the entire relative window. `per_bin_covering_trials` is accepted only for prebinned data, where exact per-bin covering-trial counts are supplied. Events mode carries no per-trial coverage records in revision 2, so the root envelope constraint refuses that combination structurally rather than inventing uniform coverage.

senderExposurePolicy.description: Required only for `mean_rate_per_selected_sender_per_trial`. The caller asserts that every selected sender was observable in every trial/bin contributing to the count and trial denominator. Cortexel retains and discloses this nonverifiable exposure assumption; it never infers exposure from whether a sender fired.

baseline.description: Optional baseline subtraction, in RELATIVE time and in the unit of `relativeWindow`. Permitted ONLY with a rate normalization: a count is extensive — it grows with the bin width and with the number of covering trials — so subtracting a mean count from bins of different width or different coverage produces a number with no consistent meaning. Both bounds must coincide with bin edges.

uncertainty.description: Revision 2 accepts no uncertainty geometry. Absence is explicit and drives UNCERTAINTY_NOT_PROVIDED; a numeric uncertainty variant is refused structurally rather than accepted and then omitted from the figure.

accessibility.summaryTemplate: Peri-event time histogram for {seriesLabel}, whose relative coordinates are aligned to {alignmentLabel} at time 0. Relative window {windowStart} to {windowStop} {timeUnit} with boundary {windowBoundary}; {zeroVisibilityStatement} {binCount} explicit bins whose individual widths and unit are retained in the complete table. {eventCount} events; selected-sender cardinality {selectedSenderCount}; included-trial cardinality {includedTrialCount}; excluded-trial cardinality {excludedTrialCount}. Denominator policy: {denominatorPolicy}. Normalization: {normalization}. Values range from {valueMin} to {valueMax} {valueUnit}. {baselineStatement} {missingBinStatement} {excludedEventStatement} {uncertaintyStatement}

tableColumns.description: Stable caller-declared series identity, repeated so a detached CSV does not rely on a mutable or colliding label.
The caller-declared series label, or the stable series id when no display label was supplied; repeated so a detached CSV remains interpretable.
The caller-declared meaning of relative time zero; unverified as an experimental fact.
Inclusive lower edge, measured from the alignment instant.
Exclusive upper edge; inclusive only for the final bin of a closed window.
Registered unit shared by binStart, binEnd, and binWidth.
The membership convention, retained independently of the bin-placement convention.
The placement convention. PSTH requires it to agree with the relative-window membership boundary.
Whether the last authoritative bin accepts its final edge. A closed relative window requires true.
Exact integer count. Null means no included trial covered this bin — it is not a measured zero. This is the raw observation everything else is derived from.
Included trials that fully cover this bin. The denominator for this bin.

Exact included cardinality, constant across bins. Under `per_bin_covering_trials` it is the ceiling the covering-trial count is read against. Prebinned mode does not retain which trial ids are included.
Exact excluded cardinality, constant across bins. Excluded trials enter no numerator or denominator. Prebinned mode does not retain which trial ids are excluded.

In events mode, events whose relative time fell outside the relative window — including one at exactly relStop under the half-open convention — and so entered no bin. Constant across bins, counted, and reported. Null in prebinned mode because no source event list exists to audit.
Exact selected cardinality, including silent selected senders. Used only by the per-sender normalization. Prebinned mode does not retain sender identities.
The closed normalization id, repeated so `Value` cannot be detached from its denominator semantics.
Null unless the per-selected-sender rate is requested; otherwise the explicit nonverifiable rectangular-exposure assertion.
The normalized value, re-derived and verified from the count, the denominator, and the bin width.
One-round exact signed difference from the aggregate baseline rate; may be negative. Null when no baseline was requested. It never replaces the count.
The closed correction id; null when no baseline was requested.
Aggregate raw-count-over-exposure baseline in valueUnit; null when absent.

externalOracle.name: elephant.statistics.time_histogram
externalOracle.notes: Elephant's time_histogram is the intended differential oracle for the uniform-denominator policy, with one SpikeTrain per trial. Its conventions must be reconciled parameter for parameter first: its `mean` output divides by the number of SPIKETRAINS, which equals the trial count only when each train pools every selected sender — the exact per-trial versus per-neuron conflation this contract splits into two normalization ids. It also requires the input trains to share t_start and t_stop, so it cannot oracle `per_bin_covering_trials` at all; that policy is covered by hand vectors only. The pinned reference environment has NOT been executed in this environment, so the status is not_run rather than assumed.

adapters.notes: Events and senders map directly. NEST has no concept of a trial: events mode still requires caller-supplied alignment times and the complete trial universe, and asserts every declared trial covered the full relative window. Partial coverage or exclusions require caller-derived prebinned counts and denominators. The adapter blocks with ADAPTER_MAPPING_REQUIRED rather than treating each simulation as one implicit trial.

One Segment may map to one events-mode trial only when every selected Segment covers the complete relative window. The alignment time must be an explicitly selected Event: a Segment's t_start is a recording boundary, not a stimulus onset, and is never used as an alignment reference by default. Unequal coverage requires prebinned mode.

The alignment column must be named explicitly; start_time is not assumed to be the aligning event. Events mode supports only a selected set of full-window trials. Exclusions or unequal trial coverage must be transformed to prebinned exact counts plus covering-trial denominators before authoring this request.
The caller must select the unit rows explicitly; the selected-sender universe is the selection, not every unit in the file.

migration.notes: The legacy payload carried a spike list and a bin width. It had no trial universe (a trial with no spikes simply vanished and shrank the denominator), no coverage (a truncated trial was indistinguishable from a silent one), and a single ambiguous `rate_hz` that could mean per-trial or per-neuron. Migration therefore REQUIRES an included-trial count and blocks with MIGRATION_INFORMATION_MISSING without one, rather than inferring the trial count from the trial ids that happen to appear. An ambiguous legacy `rate_hz` maps to the WEAKER but accurate `total_event_rate_per_trial` and emits MIGRATION_AMBIGUOUS; it is never promoted into a per-neuron claim.

changePolicy: Changing the precedence between the window boundary and the bin final-edge rule — that is, the fate of an event at exactly relStop — is MAJOR.
Changing which bin contains relative time 0 is MAJOR.
Changing any normalization formula or its denominator is MAJOR.
Changing the meaning of a normalization id, or merging the per-trial and per-neuron rate ids into one, is MAJOR.
Changing the baseline from a raw-count-and-exposure re-derivation to a mean of per-bin rates is MAJOR.
Changing the default denominator policy, or introducing any default normalization, is MAJOR.
Requiring bins to tile the window, or relaxing that requirement, is MAJOR.
Adding a normalization id, a baseline mode, an exclusion reason, or an uncertainty variant is MINOR.
knownLimitations: No BASELINE_CORRECTED disclosure id exists in disclosures.v1.json 1.0, so the baseline window, method, and derived baseline value are surfaced through the deterministic summary and the table rather than through a disclosure rule. A future registry revision should register one.

The unit registry has no `rate_change` quantity kind. A baseline-subtracted value is a signed difference of rates, carried as `firing_rate` in the declared frequency unit (Hz in the events-mode default): dimensionally correct, but the kind does not encode that it may be negative. The column is headed "Value minus baseline" so it is not read as a rate.

Baseline ratio and z-score modes are not offered. A ratio to baseline is undefined whenever the baseline count is zero — the common case for a sparse neuron — and a z-score needs a dispersion denominator the 1.0 validator registry has no entry for. Cortexel refuses rather than emit an infinity.

Revision 2 ships no PSTH compaction and no digest-bound complete table sidecar. A request above the active complete-returned-table limit is refused before derivation; bins are never merged, sampled, excerpted, or silently dropped.

Relative times are formed by one exact typed subtraction and one final rounding to binary64. Cortexel rejects overflow, underflow to an unusable exposure, and converted endpoint collapse, but finite binary64 inputs carry no metadata about the source instrument's precision; it therefore cannot establish that an otherwise representable relative time has scientifically adequate resolution for the chosen bins.

A per-bin trial denominator means the bins of one figure are not all estimated from the same trials. Each bin's denominator is in the table, but two bins with different denominators are not directly comparable as samples of one thing.
Cortexel validates that a per-bin denominator does not exceed the included-trial count; in prebinned mode it cannot verify that the denominator corresponds to trials that actually covered the bin.

Prebinned revision 2 retains exact sender and trial cardinalities but not selected-sender identities or the included/excluded membership partition of `trialIds`. Aggregate counts cannot prove those identities; PRE_BINNED_INPUT states that they are unrecoverable.

Neither raw spike rows nor prebinned counts prove that every selected sender was observable throughout every counted trial/bin. The per-selected-sender normalization therefore requires an explicit rectangular-exposure assertion and emits RECTANGULAR_SENDER_EXPOSURE_ASSERTED; heterogeneous sender exposure requires a future count-and-exposure contract.

A positive scientific bin can be narrower than the deterministic SVG coordinate grid at a requested output width. Cortexel refuses such a render with RENDER_DEGENERATE_DOMAIN rather than invisibly collapse the bin, widen it to a pixel, or overlap its neighbours; the complete numeric request remains valid for a wider output or a scientifically justified wider binning.

The trial universe is declared columnar: a flat `trialIds` array with a positionally parallel `alignmentTimes`. That is what lets ids.unique read a real array, events.trial_universe_declared read a real universe, and psth.alignment_declared read a real alignment, rather than a `/data/trials/rows/*/id` pointer the pipeline cannot resolve.

Revision 2 has no per-trial inclusion or coverage records in events mode. Consequently every declared events-mode trial is included, `excludedTrialCount` is zero, and the root request schema permits only `uniform_trial_count`. Partial coverage must use prebinned counts plus explicit per-bin denominators.

`events.within_window` is not declared for this figure: it compares absolute event times against a single absolute `/data/window`, but a PSTH's membership window is RELATIVE and resolved per trial against each alignment time. Relative-window membership and the excluded-event count are enforced at the derivation stage, which owns the alignment.

`rate.verify_normalization` is not declared for this figure: it re-derives count / (recordedSenderCount x binWidth) from a `/data/binEdges` vector — the population-rate denominator — whereas a PSTH divides by a per-bin trial denominator and keeps one authoritative bin vector at `/parameters/bins`. Supplied `rates` are re-derived at the derivation stage instead.

invalid.why: The request tries to assert that Cortexel validated it. Validation results are library-generated facts and are not caller-settable; the field is rejected, not ignored — and the authority boundary runs first, on the raw request, so this is the sole reported failure. A caller declares what its data IS, never what Cortexel concluded about it.

Events mode carries no per-trial coverage records, so it cannot compute a per-bin covering-trial denominator. Inferring coverage from which trials happened to spike would drop silent-but-observed trials and inflate the rate. The root envelope constraint therefore permits `per_bin_covering_trials` only with prebinned counts and exact supplied denominators.

The trial universe is absent: `trialIds`, `alignmentUnit`, and `alignmentTimes` are all omitted, so nothing declares which trials were run or what time zero means. Cortexel does not infer the trial count from the ids that happen to appear in the event list — a trial in which nothing happened is still a trial, and dropping it inflates every per-trial value. The events-mode branch fails structurally with the required properties missing before any denominator can be formed.

There is one event time but two event senders. The parallel arrays describe the same observations, so they must have the same length; a shorter time array does not mean "reuse the last time", it means the export is broken. series.equal_length refuses to pair a value with a time by best effort.

An event's sender is "9", which is not in the declared recorded-sender universe. A sender that was supposedly not recorded cannot have produced a recorded event, and Cortexel will not silently extend a universe the caller declared complete: doing so would either drop a real spike or enlarge the per-neuron denominator. events.sender_universe_declared rejects it.

An event declares trial t9, which is not in the declared trial universe. There is no alignment time for it, so its relative time is undefined. Dropping it silently would remove a real spike from a numerator while leaving the denominator untouched; adding t9 silently would invent a trial and enlarge the denominator. events.trial_universe_declared will not extend a universe the caller declared complete.

The trial universe declares trial t2 twice, with different alignment times. Every event of t2 would align to whichever entry the implementation read last — a 500 ms difference here, which moves every one of its spikes into a different bin — and t2 would be counted twice in the denominator. An ambiguous identity must fail before it can diverge alignment and the denominator, so ids.unique rejects it on /data/trialIds.

The relative window is inverted: start 10 is not less than stop -10, so it selects no time at all. An empty or inverted window has no membership to decide, and window.valid refuses it rather than letting a downstream bin loop silently produce nothing.

The bin edges [-10, 0, 0, 10] are not strictly increasing: the third edge repeats the second, which defines a zero-width bin that has no meaning and would silently absorb or double-count events on the boundary. bins.strictly_increasing rejects it. n edges must define n-1 half-open bins with positive width.

`ms` is canonical but `milliseconds` is only an alias, and here it is on the bin unit. A stable request must use the canonical code; the alias is rejected with a repair rather than silently converted, because a silent conversion changes the bin width — and the bin width changes the height of every peak in the figure. unit.canonical_code rejects it.

A baseline was requested with a `count` normalization. A count is extensive — it grows with the bin width and with the number of covering trials — so subtracting a mean baseline count from bins of different width or coverage produces a number with no consistent meaning. Baseline subtraction is defined only for a rate, so the parameters schema forbids `baseline` under `count` and `count_per_trial` and this fails structurally.

Revision 2 has no uncertainty geometry, table authority, or baseline-transform rule. A credible interval cannot be accepted and then omitted from the figure, so the closed parameters schema accepts only an explicit `kind: none` declaration and rejects this variant structurally.
